of #55, conda-forge is distributing `v0.20.2` of this package. Since https://github.com/xflr6/graphviz/pull/202, the `graphviz` Python package has had a floor of `python >= 3.8`. This proposes updating the recipe here to match. ## Benefits of this change Prevents `v0.20.2` from being pulled into Python 3.7 environments. `v0.20.2` contains code that is not syntactically valid Python 3.7. ```shell conda create \ --name test-env \ -c conda-forge \ --yes \ python=3.7 python-graphviz source activate test-env python -c "import graphviz" ``` ```text Traceback (most recent call last): File "", line 1, in File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/__init__.py", line 31, in from .backend import (DOT_BINARY, UNFLATTEN_BINARY, File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/__init__.py", line 5, in from .mixins import Render, Pipe, Unflatten, View File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/mixins.py", line 8, in from . import piping File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/piping.py", line 5, in from .. import _tools File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/_tools.py", line 23 def attach(object: typing.Any, /, name: str) -> typing.Callable: ^ SyntaxError: invalid syntax ``` ## Checklist * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [ ] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [x] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Notes for Reviewers Noticed this in CI for `lightgbm`, where we are still trying to do some testing with Python 3.7 and to use conda-forge to create environments: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/6370. I'm not proposing a repodata patch, as I know conda-forge is no longer supporting Python 3.7 (https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge-repodata-patches-feedstock/pull/478#pullrequestreview-1523437604). But hope this PR could be merged... I think it'd be a low-risk way to prevent others from encountering this import error. Thanks for your time and CLA"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-04-30T15:26:49Z","updated_at":"2024-07-22T04:03:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/pulls/5322","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"### Description

Fixes #5323

"Use Environment Variables" in the `conda-build` docs says the following about variables in `build.script_env` in recipes (my emphasis)

> _If a listed environment variable is missing from the environment seen by the conda-build process itself, a UserWarning is emitted during the build process **and the variable remains undefined**._

([link](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda-build/en/stable/resources/define-metadata.html#use-environment-variables))

As of #5105, `conda-build` does not behave that way for multi-output recipes. As of that PR, any variable mentioned in the recipe's `build.script_env` but not found in the environment where `conda-build` is called from will be set to the empty string `''` in the build script environment.

This PR proposes ensuring that such variables remain unset in the build script environment.

Thanks for your time and consideration.

## Benefits of these changes

Removes a source of behavioral difference between top-level and multi-output builds.

Reduces a source of friction when using build scripts that are sensitive to the difference between environment variables being unset and being set to an empty string. ([for example, with optional AWS credentials for `sccache` like this](https://github.com/mozilla/sccache/blob/4e79d9e78e127e2bfe7bb7a8f170b3a99b549941/src/config.rs#L659-L664)).

### Checklist - did you ...

- [x] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [x] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

### How I tested this

The unit test I've added here fails on `main`.

```shell
git checkout main -- conda_build/build.py
pytest tests/test_subpackages.py::test_build_script_does_not_set_env_from_script_env_if_missing
# Failed: DID NOT RAISE <class 'subprocess.CalledProcessError'>
```

And prints this message from the build script in stdout of the build process, showing that the unset environment variable is set to `''`.

```text
val... 
```

But passes on this branch.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/5322/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/5322/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/comments/2241999141","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322#issuecomment-2241999141","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/5322","id":2241999141,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAPRE586FojEl","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-22T04:03:57Z","updated_at":"2024-07-22T04:03:57Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I've updated this to latest `main`.

CLA"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-04-30T15:26:49Z","updated_at":"2024-06-04T01:05:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/pulls/5322","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"### Description

Fixes #5323

"Use Environment Variables" in the `conda-build` docs says the following about variables in `build.script_env` in recipes (my emphasis)

> _If a listed environment variable is missing from the environment seen by the conda-build process itself, a UserWarning is emitted during the build process **and the variable remains undefined**._

([link](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda-build/en/stable/resources/define-metadata.html#use-environment-variables))

As of #5105, `conda-build` does not behave that way for multi-output recipes. As of that PR, any variable mentioned in the recipe's `build.script_env` but not found in the environment where `conda-build` is called from will be set to the empty string `''` in the build script environment.

This PR proposes ensuring that such variables remain unset in the build script environment.

Thanks for your time and consideration.

## Benefits of these changes

Removes a source of behavioral difference between top-level and multi-output builds.

Reduces a source of friction when using build scripts that are sensitive to the difference between environment variables being unset and being set to an empty string. ([for example, with optional AWS credentials for `sccache` like this](https://github.com/mozilla/sccache/blob/4e79d9e78e127e2bfe7bb7a8f170b3a99b549941/src/config.rs#L659-L664)).

### Checklist - did you ...

- [x] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [x] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

### How I tested this

The unit test I've added here fails on `main`.

```shell
git checkout main -- conda_build/build.py
pytest tests/test_subpackages.py::test_build_script_does_not_set_env_from_script_env_if_missing
# Failed: DID NOT RAISE <class 'subprocess.CalledProcessError'>
```

And prints this message from the build script in stdout of the build process, showing that the unset environment variable is set to `''`.

```text
val... 
```

But passes on this branch.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/5322/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/5322/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/comments/2146376719","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322#issuecomment-2146376719","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/5322","id":2146376719,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAPRE585_7xwP","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-04T01:05:31Z","updated_at":"2024-06-04T01:05:31Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"As of #5264, a codepath that was raising `subprocess.CalledProcessError` started raising `conda_build.exceptions.BuildScriptException`, breaking the unit test I'd added here.

Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

X = np.random.default_rng().uniform(size=(10, 2)).astype(np.float32)
df = pd.DataFrame(X)
df_arr = df.to_numpy(dtype=np.float32)

np.shares_memory(X, df.values)
# numpy==1.26.4    , pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==3.0.0.dev0+1067 : False

np.shares_memory(X, df_arr)
# numpy==1.26.4    , pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==3.0.0.dev0+1067 : False
```


### Issue Description

Starting with `pandas==3.0.0`, it appears that `DataFrame(data)` creates a copy when `data` is a `numpy` array.

### Expected Behavior

I expected `df.values` and the result of `df.to_numpy()` (with `copy` argument omitted) to return the same `numpy` array that the `DataFrame` was created from.

I think that that has been the behavior of most combinations of `pandas` and `numpy` for at least the last 2 years. I think that because we've been running a test in LightGBM with similar code, to confirm that `lightgbm` isn't creating unnecessary copies in its `pandas` support since January 2022 (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4927), and that test is now failing with `pandas>=3.0.0`.

Apologies in advance if this is intentional behavior. I did try to look through the `git` blame, issues, and PRs. Did not see anything in these possibly-related discussions:

* https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/58243
* https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/56022
* https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/52823

### Installed Versions

Observed this in a `conda` environment on an M2 macbook, using Python 3.11.9

Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

X = np.random.default_rng().uniform(size=(10, 2)).astype(np.float32)
df = pd.DataFrame(X)
df_arr = df.to_numpy(dtype=np.float32)

np.shares_memory(X, df.values)
# numpy==1.26.4    , pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==3.0.0.dev0+1067 : False

np.shares_memory(X, df_arr)
# numpy==1.26.4    , pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==2.2.2           : True
# numpy==2.1.0.dev0, pandas==3.0.0.dev0+1067 : False
```


### Issue Description

Starting with `pandas==3.0.0`, it appears that `DataFrame(data)` creates a copy when `data` is a `numpy` array.

### Expected Behavior

I expected `df.values` and the result of `df.to_numpy()` (with `copy` argument omitted) to return the same `numpy` array that the `DataFrame` was created from.

I think that that has been the behavior of most combinations of `pandas` and `numpy` for at least the last 2 years. I think that because we've been running a test in LightGBM with similar code, to confirm that `lightgbm` isn't creating unnecessary copies in its `pandas` support since January 2022 (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4927), and that test is now failing with `pandas>=3.0.0`.

Apologies in advance if this is intentional behavior. I did try to look through the `git` blame, issues, and PRs. Did not see anything in these possibly-related discussions:

* https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/58243
* https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/56022
* https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/52823

### Installed Versions

Observed this in a `conda` environment on an M2 macbook, using Python 3.11.9

In the most recent (v4.4.0) release, the Python package there picked up a runtime floor on `numpy` (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/6468). I came here to add that floor to the `conda-forge` package, and found a few other opportunities to align the pins here with the way `lightgbm` is packaged for PyPI. This proposes: * adding floors on `cmake`, `numpy`, and `python` * removing unnecessary host dependency on `setuptools` * adding `run_constrained:` floors for `lightgbm`'s optional dependencies For reference, these all mirror the dependencies in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/master/python-package/pyproject.toml. Thanks for your time and consideration. ### Checklist * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [ ] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. hi, I'm one of the maintainers of LightGBM. In the most recent (v4.4.0) release, the Python package there picked up a runtime floor on `numpy` (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/6468). I came here to add that floor to the `conda-forge` package, and found a few other opportunities to align the pins here with the way `lightgbm` is packaged for PyPI. This proposes: * adding floors on `cmake` and `numpy` * removing unnecessary host dependency on `setuptools` * adding `run_constrained:` floors for `lightgbm`'s optional dependencies For reference, these all mirror the dependencies in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/master/python-package/pyproject.toml. Thanks for your time and consideration. ### Checklist * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [ ] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. Comment:

I'm interested in comparing CPU vs. GPU performance across some of the popular ML libraries. I went to set up a conda environment to do this and realized that although ``lightgbm`` implements GPU support, this feedstock isn't building the package with it enabled.

conda-forge has other feedstocks that're building both ``_cpu`` and ``_cuda`` versions of packages. For example:
* [conda-forge/arrow-cpp-feedstock](https://github.com/conda-forge/arrow-cpp-feedstock)
* [conda-forge/xgboost-feedstock](https://github.com/conda-forge/xgboost-feedstock)

It'd be great to have this feedstock building for both CPU and GPU in the same way.

Thanks!

N.B. related"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-02-01T06:51:11Z","updated_at":"2024-05-26T05:43:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the workflow you want to enable

I would like to propose an enhancement to the `check_estimator` function, particularly in light of the implementation of SLEP007. As per SLEP007, which was integrated into scikit-learn starting from version 1.1.0, estimators are expected to support `feature_names_in_` and `n_features_in_` attributes. However, it appears that the `check_estimator` utility does not currently enforce the presence of these attributes.

### Describe your proposed solution

I propose that the `check_estimator` function be updated to include checks for the existence of the `feature_names_in_` and `n_features_in_` attributes.

### Additional context

- SLEP007:[ https://scikit-learn-enhancement-proposals.readthedocs.io/en/latest/slep007/proposal.html](https://scikit-learn-enhancement-proposals.readthedocs.io/en/latest/slep007/proposal.html)
- Version of scikit-learn where SLEP007 was implemented: 1.1.0
- Example of `check_estimator` not enforcing these attributes: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/6279","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2132074286","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337#issuecomment-2132074286","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337","id":2132074286,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAzd1s5_FN8u","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-26T05:43:28Z","updated_at":"2024-05-26T05:43:28Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Thanks very much for reading through this whole thread and for that response.

Dependency Analysis -------------------- Please note that this analysis is **highly experimental**. The aim here is to make maintenance easier by inspecting the package's dependencies. Feature","color":"02e10c","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-02-01T06:51:11Z","updated_at":"2024-04-15T02:21:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the workflow you want to enable

I would like to propose an enhancement to the `check_estimator` function, particularly in light of the implementation of SLEP007. As per SLEP007, which was integrated into scikit-learn starting from version 1.1.0, estimators are expected to support `feature_names_in_` and `n_features_in_` attributes. However, it appears that the `check_estimator` utility does not currently enforce the presence of these attributes.

### Describe your proposed solution

I propose that the `check_estimator` function be updated to include checks for the existence of the `feature_names_in_` and `n_features_in_` attributes.

### Additional context

- SLEP007:[ https://scikit-learn-enhancement-proposals.readthedocs.io/en/latest/slep007/proposal.html](https://scikit-learn-enhancement-proposals.readthedocs.io/en/latest/slep007/proposal.html)
- Version of scikit-learn where SLEP007 was implemented: 1.1.0
- Example of `check_estimator` not enforcing these attributes: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/6279","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2054396747","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337#issuecomment-2054396747","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28337","id":2054396747,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAzd1s56c5tL","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-15T02:21:23Z","updated_at":"2024-04-15T02:21:23Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Thanks @nicklamiller . Do you have link(s) to share for that claim?

Before we make changes in `lightgbm` (and maybe go propose them in other projects like XGBoost), I'd really like to hear an authoritative answer from a `scikit-learn` maintainer about what the expectation is for estimators like `lightgbm.LGBMClassifier` which inherit from `BaseEstimator` and which implement `fit()` but not `transform()`.

* should such estimators implement `feature_names_in`?
* should they implement `_validate_data()`?
* is there some documentation besides https://scikit-learn-enhancement-proposals.readthedocs.io/en/latest/slep007/proposal.html that could be used to confirm an implementation complies with `scikit-learn`'s expectations?

I'm happy to help with the work to keep `lightgbm`'s scikit-learn estimators compliant with changes here but I'm finding it difficult to understand the precise expectations.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2054396747/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":5322,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/pulls/5322","id":1848214135,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAPRE585uKYJ3","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/5322.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/5322","number":5322,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"ensure undefined script_env variables are undefined in multi-output build environment","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"### Description

"Use Environment Variables" in the `conda-build` docs says the following about variables in `build.script_env` in recipes (my emphasis)

> _If a listed environment variable is missing from the environment seen by the conda-build process itself, a UserWarning is emitted during the build process **and the variable remains undefined**._

([link](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda-build/en/stable/resources/define-metadata.html#use-environment-variables))

As of #5105, `conda-build` does not behave that way for multi-output recipes. As of that PR, any variable mentioned in the recipe's `build.script_env` but not found in the environment where `conda-build` is called from will be set to the empty string `''` in the build script environment.

This PR proposes ensuring that such variables remain unset in the build script environment.

Thanks for your time and consideration.

## Benefits of these changes

Removes a source of behavioral difference between top-level and multi-output builds.

Reduces a source of friction when using build scripts that are sensitive to the difference between environment variables being unset and being set to an empty string. ([for example, with optional AWS credentials for `sccache` like this](https://github.com/mozilla/sccache/blob/4e79d9e78e127e2bfe7bb7a8f170b3a99b549941/src/config.rs#L659-L664)).

### Checklist - did you ...

- [ ] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [x] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

### How I tested this

The unit test I've added here fails on `main`.

```shell
git checkout main -- conda_build/build.py
pytest tests/test_subpackages.py::test_build_script_does_not_set_env_from_script_env_if_missing
# Failed: DID NOT RAISE <class 'subprocess.CalledProcessError'>
```

And prints this message from the build script in stdout of the build process, showing that the unset environment variable is set to `''`.

```text
val... 
```

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open reports and couldn't find a duplicate

### What happened?

"Use Environment Variables" in the `conda-build` docs says the following about variables in `build.script_env` in recipes (my emphasis)

> _If a listed environment variable is missing from the environment seen by the conda-build process itself, a UserWarning is emitted during the build process **and the variable remains undefined**._

([link](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda-build/en/stable/resources/define-metadata.html#use-environment-variables))

As of #5105, `conda-build` does not behave that way for multi-output recipes. As of that PR, any variable mentioned in the recipe's `build.script_env` but not found in the environment where `conda-build` is called from will be set to the empty string `''` in the build script environment.

For example, with a recipe like this:

```yaml
# meta.yaml
package:
  name: testpkg
  version: 1.0

outputs:
  - name: test_subpackage_1
    build:
      script_env:
        - SPARKLY_UNICORN
      script:
        - python -c 'import os; print("val... " + os.environ["SPARKLY_UNICORN"])'
      requirements:
        host:
          - python
```

I'd expect that build script to fail if run in an environment without environment variable `SPARKLY_UNICORN` set.

```shell
env -u SPARKLY_UNICORN conda-build testpkg
```

Instead, it succeeds, because environment variable `SPARKLY_UNICORN` is defined and set to the empty string `''` in the build script environment.

Description

Fixes #5323

"Use Environment Variables" in the `conda-build` docs says the following about variables in `build.script_env` in recipes (my emphasis)

> _If a listed environment variable is missing from the environment seen by the conda-build process itself, a UserWarning is emitted during the build process **and the variable remains undefined**._

([link](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda-build/en/stable/resources/define-metadata.html#use-environment-variables))

As of #5105, `conda-build` does not behave that way for multi-output recipes. As of that PR, any variable mentioned in the recipe's `build.script_env` but not found in the environment where `conda-build` is called from will be set to the empty string `''` in the build script environment.

This PR proposes ensuring that such variables remain unset in the build script environment.

Thanks for your time and consideration.

## Benefits of these changes

Removes a source of behavioral difference between top-level and multi-output builds.

Reduces a source of friction when using build scripts that are sensitive to the difference between environment variables being unset and being set to an empty string. ([for example, with optional AWS credentials for `sccache` like this](https://github.com/mozilla/sccache/blob/4e79d9e78e127e2bfe7bb7a8f170b3a99b549941/src/config.rs#L659-L664)).

### Checklist - did you ...

- [x] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [x] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

### How I tested this

The unit test I've added here fails on `main`.

```shell
git checkout main -- conda_build/build.py
pytest tests/test_subpackages.py::test_build_script_does_not_set_env_from_script_env_if_missing
# Failed: DID NOT RAISE <class 'subprocess.CalledProcessError'>
```

And prints this message from the build script in stdout of the build process, showing that the unset environment variable is set to `''`.

```text
val... 
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11006","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11006/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11006/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11006/events","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/11006","id":2190464223,"node_id":"I_kwDOAbcwm86Cj9Tf","number":11006,"title":"as of v2024.3.1, comparing a 1D dask.array.Array to a dask.dataframe.Series fails","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-03-17T03:47:01Z","updated_at":"2024-03-17T03:47:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Describe the issue**:

Prior to `dask < 2024.3.0`, it was possible to directly combined a 1-dimensional Dask Array and a Dask Series with operators like `==`, `-`, and `+`, like this:

```python
(d_ser == d_arr).compute()
```

As of `v2024.3.1`, with the switch to `dask-expr`, that instead raises an exception like this:

```text
ValueError: For a 1d array, columns must be a scalar or single element list
```

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

Consider the following Python code.

```python
import dask.array as da
import dask.dataframe as dd
import pandas as pd

d_series = dd.from_pandas(pd.Series([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0]), npartitions=1)
d_array = d_series.to_dask_array()

(d_series == d_array).compute()
(d_series - d_array).compute()
(d_series + d_array).compute()
```

In a Python 3.11 environment with `dask==2024.2.1`, `numpy==1.26.4`, and `pandas==2.2.1`, that succeeds.

```python
(d_series == d_array).compute()
# 0    True
# 1    True
# 2    True
# 3    True
# dtype: bool
```

In a Python 3.11 environment with `dask==2024.3.1`, that latest `dask-expr` (1.0.3), and the same `numpy` and `pandas` versions, it fails.

```text
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/dask_expr/_collection.py", line 160, in _wrap_expr_op
    other = from_dask_array(
            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/dask_expr/_collection.py", line 4730, in from_dask_array
    df = from_dask_array(x, columns=columns, index=index, meta=meta)
         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/dask/dataframe/io/io.py", line 433, in from_dask_array
    meta = _meta_from_array(x, columns, index, meta=meta)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/dask/dataframe/io/io.py", line 86, in _meta_from_array
    raise ValueError(
ValueError: For a 1d array, columns must be a scalar or single element list
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:

I've read #10995. I don't believe this issue is already captured elsewhere here or at https://github.com/dask/dask-expr/issues.

I discovered this behavior change because it broke LightGBM's CI: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/6365. It was convenient there to be able to compare two 1-dimensional arrays to each other and not need to care whether one was a dask Array and another was a Dask Series, but if that's a bad practice or no longer supported here, it won't be a huge problem to change it. I don't know if other projects rely on that behavior though.

Note that `pandas` and `numpy` objects can be compared in these ways (as of `pandas==2.2.1` and `numpy==1.26.4`):

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

ser = pd.Series([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0])
arr = ser.values

ser == arr
arr == ser
```

**Environment**:

I set these up using the official `python:3.11` container images.

```shell
docker run --rm -it python:3.11 bash
```

Latest `dask`, `numpy`, `pandas`, and `dask-expr`:

```shell
docker run --rm -it python:3.11 bash
pip install \
    'dask==2024.3.1' \
    'distributed==2024.3.1' \
    'dask-expr==1.0.3' \
    'numpy==1.26.4' \
    'pandas==2.2.1'
```

Slightly older `dask` and the newest `dask-expr` that supported it:

```shell
docker run --rm -it python:3.11 bash
pip install \
    'dask==2024.2.1' \
    'distributed==2024.2.1' \
    'dask-expr==0.5.3' \
    'numpy==1.26.4' \
    'pandas==2.2.1'
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11006/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11006/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":11007,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/pulls/11007","id":1775911632,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAbcwm85p2kLQ","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/11007","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/11007.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/11007.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/11007","number":11007,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"raise ImportError instead of ValueError when dask-expr cannot be imported","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"#10634 introduced an import-time check that ensures that a statement like this:

```python
import dask.dataframe
```

will raise an exception under the following conditions:

* a sufficiently-new version of `pandas` exists in the environment
* Dask config `"dataframe.query-planning"` is `True`
* `dask_expr` cannot be imported

That exception is currently a `ValueError`. This proposes changing it to an `ImportError`.

### Benefits of this change

Allows the use of this pattern to conditionally import `dask.dataframe`:

```python
try:
    import dask.dataframe
except ImportError:
    # ... do something if loading dask.dataframe failed ...
```

That's the same pattern `dask` itself uses to conditionally import dependencies, for example:

https://github.com/dask/dask/blob/f9310c440b661f1ae96ffd8726404d3c3fe32edc/dask/dataframe/__init__.py#L83-L86

And I think it's common enough across other Python projects that Dask should support it unless there's a compelliing reason to use `ValueError` instead. Some examples:

* `prefect` ([code link](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/b40569482ec269b20d4b89110e818cc764bc14cd/src/prefect/_internal/pytz.py#L16-L21))
* `scikit-learn` ([code link](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/082b5884faccbcc5509a64a347fec55788b2f984/sklearn/utils/fixes.py#L64-L69))
* `xgboost` ([code link](https://github.com/dmlc/xgboost/blob/53fc17578f88a33e6265772837676cdf911f793e/python-package/xgboost/compat.py#L33-L43))

### Notes for Reviewers

I think (but don't know with certainty) that this change would help other projects that rely on `dask.dataframe` but aren't yet supporting `dask-expr`. It might help with  https://github.com/dask/dask-expr/issues/904, for example.

I'm certain it would help `lightgbm`. `dask` is an optional dependency of that library, but as of `dask==2024.3.1`, this use of `ValueError` would break `import lightgbm` *even if you do not intend to use any of LightGBM's Dask features*.

```shell
docker run \
    --rm \
    -v $(pwd):/opt/LightGBM \
    -w /opt/LightGBM \
    -it python:3.11 \
    bash

pip install \
    'dask==2024.3.1' \
    'lightgbm>=4.3.0' \
    'pandas==2.2.1' \
    'pyyaml'

DASK_DATAFRAME__QUERY_PLANNING=true \
python -c "import lightgbm"
```

{"action":"opened","number":56,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/python-graphviz-feedstock/pulls/56","id":1776460029,"node_id":"PR_kwDOBIE0gc5p4qD9","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/python-graphviz-feedstock/pull/56","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/python-graphviz-feedstock/pull/56.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/python-graphviz-feedstock/pull/56.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/python-graphviz-feedstock/issues/56","number":56,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"bump to python >=3.8","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"As of #55, conda-forge is distributing `v0.20.2` of this package. Since https://github.com/xflr6/graphviz/pull/202, the `graphviz` Python package has had a floor of `python >= 3.8`. This proposes updating the recipe here to match. ## Benefits of this change Prevents `v0.20.2` from being pulled into Python 3.7 environments. `v0.20.2` contains code that is not syntactically valid Python 3.7. ```shell conda create \ --name test-env \ -c conda-forge \ --yes \ python=3.7 python-graphviz source activate test-env python -c "import graphviz" ``` ```text Traceback (most recent call last): File "", line 1, in File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/__init__.py", line 31, in from .backend import (DOT_BINARY, UNFLATTEN_BINARY, File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/__init__.py", line 5, in from .mixins import Render, Pipe, Unflatten, View File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/mixins.py", line 8, in from . import piping File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/piping.py", line 5, in from .. import _tools File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/_tools.py", line 23 def attach(object: typing.Any, /, name: str) -> typing.Callable: ^ SyntaxError: invalid syntax ``` ## Checklist * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [ ] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [ ] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [ ] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Notes for Reviewers Noticed this in CI for `lightgbm`, where we are still trying to do some testing with Python 3.7 and to use conda-forge to create environments: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/6370. I'm not proposing a repodata patch, as I know conda-forge is no longer supporting Python 3.7 (https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge-repodata-patches-feedstock/pull/478#pullrequestreview-1523437604). But hope this PR could be merged... I think it'd be a low-risk way to prevent others from encountering this import error. Thanks for your time and of #55, conda-forge is distributing `v0.20.2` of this package. Since https://github.com/xflr6/graphviz/pull/202, the `graphviz` Python package has had a floor of `python >= 3.8`. This proposes updating the recipe here to match. ## Benefits of this change Prevents `v0.20.2` from being pulled into Python 3.7 environments. `v0.20.2` contains code that is not syntactically valid Python 3.7. ```shell conda create \ --name test-env \ -c conda-forge \ --yes \ python=3.7 python-graphviz source activate test-env python -c "import graphviz" ``` ```text Traceback (most recent call last): File "", line 1, in File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/__init__.py", line 31, in from .backend import (DOT_BINARY, UNFLATTEN_BINARY, File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/__init__.py", line 5, in from .mixins import Render, Pipe, Unflatten, View File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/mixins.py", line 8, in from . import piping File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/backend/piping.py", line 5, in from .. import _tools File "/Users/jlamb/miniforge/envs/test-env/lib/python3.7/site-packages/graphviz/_tools.py", line 23 def attach(object: typing.Any, /, name: str) -> typing.Callable: ^ SyntaxError: invalid syntax ``` ## Checklist * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [ ] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [ ] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [ ] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Notes for Reviewers Noticed this in CI for `lightgbm`, where we are still trying to do some testing with Python 3.7 and to use conda-forge to create environments: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/6370. I'm not proposing a repodata patch, as I know conda-forge is no longer supporting Python 3.7 (https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge-repodata-patches-feedstock/pull/478#pullrequestreview-1523437604). But hope this PR could be merged... I think it'd be a low-risk way to prevent others from encountering this import error. Thanks for your time and Feature","color":"02e10c","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-02-01T06:51:11Z","updated_at":"2024-03-29T04:30:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the workflow you want to enable

I would like to propose an enhancement to the `check_estimator` function, particularly in light of the implementation of SLEP007. As per SLEP007, which was integrated into scikit-learn starting from version 1.1.0, estimators are expected to support `feature_names_in_` and `n_features_in_` attributes. However, it appears that the `check_estimator` utility does not currently enforce the presence of these attributes.

### Describe your proposed solution

I propose that the `check_estimator` function be updated to include checks for the existence of the `feature_names_in_` and `n_features_in_` attributes.

### Additional context

- SLEP007:[ https://scikit-learn-enhancement-proposals.readthedocs.io/en/latest/slep007/proposal.html](https://scikit-learn-enhancement-proposals.readthedocs.io/en/latest/slep007/proposal.html)
- Version of scikit-learn where SLEP007 was implemented: 1.1.0

Are you sure that is working as expected? `lightgbm`'s scikit-learn estimators inherit from `BaseEstimator` and don't seem to be getting `feature_names_in_` defined.

Consider this example with Python 3.11, `lightgbm==4.3.0`, and `scikit-learn==1.4.1`.

```python
import lightgbm as lgb
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits

X, y = load_digits(n_class=2, return_X_y=True, as_frame=True)

# prove that this dataframe has column names
print(X.columns[:5])
# 'pixel_0_0', 'pixel_0_1', 'pixel_0_2', 'pixel_0_3', ...

# construct the estimator + fit the model
est = lgb.LGBMModel(n_estimators=5, objective="binary")
est.fit(X, y)

# try to get 'feature_names_in_'
est.feature_names_in_
# AttributeError: 'LGBMModel' object has no attribute 'feature_names_in_'.

# but it does inherit from BaseEstimator!
est.__class__.__mro__
# (<class 'lightgbm.sklearn.LGBMModel'>, <class 'sklearn.base.BaseEstimator'>, <class 'sklearn.utils._estimator_html_repr._HTMLDocumentationLinkMixin'>, <class 'sklearn.utils._metadata_requests._MetadataRequester'>, <class 'object'>)
```

What am I misunderstanding? Does every estimator need to explicitly implement this as `xgboost` did in https://github.com/dmlc/xgboost/pull/7526?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2026628918/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Summary This PR proposes running `{data.table}`'s unit tests as a part of builds here. This ~introduces 0 new build-time dependencies and~ offers significantly higher coverage of potential build problems than the current testing strategy, which is "check that `library(data.table)` runs without error". Let's see what the change in build time is when CI runs, but assuming it's on the order of 0-3 minutes, I think it's worth it in exchange for stronger guarantees about correctness of the build here. Especially for a project like `{data.table}`, which is depended on by hundreds of other R packages (see "reverse imports" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html). ## How I tested this Locally on my mac (intel chip, macOS 12.2.1, 8GB RAM), ran the following on this branch: ```shell docker run \ --rm \ -v $(pwd):/opt/data.table \ -w /opt/data.table \ --entrypoint="" \ -it condaforge/linux-anvil-cos7-x86_64 \ bash conda build . ``` Saw that build succeed, and this output confirming that the tests ran successsfully: ```text All 8997 tests (last 2163) in tests/tests.Rraw.bz2 completed ok in 25.8s elapsed (28.2s cpu) + exit 0 Resource usage statistics from testing r-data.table: Process count: 4 CPU time: Sys=0:00:00.8, User=0:00:27.6 Memory: 177.3M Disk usage: 16B Time elapsed: 0:00:28.1 ``` For details on that `test.data.table()` function, see https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/reference/test.data.table.html?q=test.data#ref-examples. Thanks very much for your time and consideration.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/issues/35/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/issues/35/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/issues/comments/1874788139","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/pull/35#issuecomment-1874788139","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/issues/35","id":1874788139,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBTvHfc5vvv8r","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-03T02:50:50Z","updated_at":"2024-01-03T02:50:50Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> I wonder if that directory is maybe missing under `--without-internal-tzcode`? Looks like it is missing. In a `conda` environment with `conda-forge`'s R 4.2, I see the following. ```shell env -i PATH=/usr/bin:/bin LANG=en_US.UTF-8 \ "$CONDA_PREFIX/bin/Rscript" -e 'print(file.path(R.home("share"), "zoneinfo"))' # "/Users/jlamb/mambaforge/envs/r42/lib/R/share/zoneinfo" ls /Users/jlamb/mambaforge/envs/r42/lib/R/share/zoneinfo # ls: /Users/jlamb/mambaforge/envs/r42/lib/R/share/zoneinfo: No such file or directory find $CONDA_PREFIX -name 'zoneinfo' -type d # (empty) ``` > Perhaps conda needs to supply `TZDIR`= to point to a specific conda-internal timezone info database? Yeah, to work with these pre-1900 dates, it would be better to point R at some other suitable set of timezone information via `TZDIR`. For example, the IANA timezone database is packaged for `conda-forge` at https://github.com/conda-forge/tzdata-feedstock. I tried installing it and pointing R at it via `TZIDR`. That unfortunately did not work. ```shell conda install --yes -n r42 tzcode tzdata find $CONDA_PREFIX -name 'zoneinfo' -type d # /Users/jlamb/mambaforge/envs/r42/share/zoneinfo env -i PATH=/usr/bin:/bin \ "$CONDA_PREFIX/bin/Rscript" -e 'as.POSIXct("1893-12-28 05:15:36", tz = "")' # Error in as.POSIXlt.character(x, tz, ...) : # character string is not in a standard unambiguous format env -i PATH=/usr/bin:/bin LANG=en_US.UTF-8 TZDIR="$CONDA_PREFIX/share/zoneinfo" \ "$CONDA_PREFIX/bin/Rscript" -e 'as.POSIXct("1893-12-28 05:15:36", tz = "")' # Error in as.POSIXlt.character(x, tz, ...) : # character string is not in a standard unambiguous format ``` I do see a timezone file for my timezone there. ```shell env -i PATH=/usr/bin:/bin LANG=en_US.UTF-8 TZDIR="$CONDA_PREFIX/share/zoneinfo" \ "$CONDA_PREFIX/bin/Rscript" -e 'print(Sys.timezone())' # [1] "America/Chicago" ls $CONDA_PREFIX/share/zoneinfo/America ``` So maybe there is more going on here?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/issues/comments/1874788139/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

But I also don't think that should block improving `conda-forge`'s `data.table` testing... the patch you put up in https://github.com/Rdatatable/data.table/pull/5867 shoulld be enough for that. CRAN's","user":{"login":"jameslamb","id":7608904,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc2MDg5MDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7608904?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb","html_url":"https://github.com/jameslamb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jameslamb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":501660533,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw1MDE2NjA1MzM=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/r-base-feedstock/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"ee0701","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-03T03:20:35Z","updated_at":"2024-01-03T03:20:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

On R 4.2.3 for macOS x86_84 installed from CRAN (https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/), the following works as expected.

```shell
env -i PATH=/usr/bin:/bin \
/usr/local/bin/Rscript -e 'as.POSIXct("1893-12-28 05:15:36", tz = "")'
# [1] "1893-12-28 05:15:36 CST"
```

On `conda-forge`'s R 4.2.3 for macOS x86_64, that fails.

```shell
conda create \
    --yes \
    --name r42 \
    'r-base==4.2.3'

source activate r42

env -i PATH=/usr/bin:/bin \
"$CONDA_PREFIX/bin/Rscript" -e 'as.POSIXct("1893-12-28 05:15:36", tz = "")'
# Error in as.POSIXlt.character(x, tz, ...) : 
#  character string is not in a standard unambiguous format
# Calls: as.POSIXct ... as.POSIXct.default -> as.POSIXct -> as.POSIXlt -> as.POSIXlt.character
```

I was not able to reproduce that difference on Linux, Windows, or arm64 macOS. I was not able to reproduce that on R 4.3.x.

### Investigation

I believe that's because `r-base` for macOS is compiled with `--without-internal-tzcode`.

https://github.com/conda-forge/r-base-feedstock/blob/c4525e474a162db58b978f5ed83f051b8ab07c78/recipe/build-r-base.sh#L529

As described at https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-devel/R-admin.html (my emphasis)

> *Option `--with-internal-tzcode` makes use of R’s own code and copy of the IANA database for managing timezones. This will be preferred where there are issues with the system implementation, usually involving times after 2037 or **before 1916**. An alternative time-zone directory[10](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-devel/R-admin.html#FOOT10) can be used, pointed to by environment variable TZDIR: this should contain files such as Europe/London. On all tested OSes the system timezone was deduced correctly, but if necessary it can be set as the value of environment variable TZ.*

> *configure defaults to `--with-internal-tzcode` on macOS. The native implementation used to be unusable on earlier versions (with a 32-bit time_t and/or timezone tables missing information beyond the 32-bit range). For, e.g., macOS 12.6, option --without-internal-tzcode can be used to override this and R contains sufficient workarounds (for example, the **native code fails to recognize dates with a negative tm_year, that is dates before 1900**) for R to pass its checks. However, there are discrepancies, notably in Europe in the 1900s and 1940s, even though the Olson database contains the correct information.*

`conda-forge`'s build of `r-base` differs from R's default, and passes `--without-internal-tzcode` on macOS ([code link](https://github.com/conda-forge/r-base-feedstock/blob/c4525e474a162db58b978f5ed83f051b8ab07c78/recipe/build-r-base.sh#L529)).

According to `git blame`, it's been like that since the commit that first introduced that recipe 7 years ago ([blame link](https://github.com/conda-forge/r-base-feedstock/blame/c4525e474a162db58b978f5ed83f051b8ab07c78/recipe/build-r-base.sh#L529)).

### References

These conclusions are supported by investigation @MichaelChirico and I recently did into a change I proposed for `{data.table}`'s feedstock:

* https://github.com/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/pull/35#issuecomment-1872477543
* https://github.com/Rdatatable/data.table/pull/5867

Not sure, but this might be the cause of what @jdblischak experienced in https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/pull/5405#issuecomment-373718358.

Note that the IANA timezone database (the one R also bundles) is packaged in 2 different formats for `conda-forge`:

* https://github.com/conda-forge/tzdata-feedstock
* https://github.com/conda-forge/r-tzdb-feedstock

### Notes for Reviewers

I appreciate that any change to how `r-base` is compiled can potentially have a huge impact. I'm not as familiar with `conda-forge` as others here, but I'd be happy to help however I can with other work to make this change happen or provide more evidence that it should happen.

Thanks for your time and consideration.

# Calls: as.POSIXct ... as.POSIXct.default -> as.POSIXct -> as.POSIXlt -> as.POSIXlt.character
```

I was not able to reproduce that difference on Linux, Windows, or arm64 macOS. I was not able to reproduce that on R 4.3.x.

### Investigation

I believe that's because `r-base` for macOS is compiled with `--without-internal-tzcode`.

https://github.com/conda-forge/r-base-feedstock/blob/c4525e474a162db58b978f5ed83f051b8ab07c78/recipe/build-r-base.sh#L529

As described at https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-devel/R-admin.html (my emphasis)

> *Option `--with-internal-tzcode` makes use of R’s own code and copy of the IANA database for managing timezones. This will be preferred where there are issues with the system implementation, usually involving times after 2037 or **before 1916**. An alternative time-zone directory[10](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-devel/R-admin.html#FOOT10) can be used, pointed to by environment variable TZDIR: this should contain files such as Europe/London. On all tested OSes the system timezone was deduced correctly, but if necessary it can be set as the value of environment variable TZ.*

> *configure defaults to `--with-internal-tzcode` on macOS. The native implementation used to be unusable on earlier versions (with a 32-bit time_t and/or timezone tables missing information beyond the 32-bit range). For, e.g., macOS 12.6, option --without-internal-tzcode can be used to override this and R contains sufficient workarounds (for example, the **native code fails to recognize dates with a negative tm_year, that is dates before 1900**) for R to pass its checks. However, there are discrepancies, notably in Europe in the 1900s and 1940s, even though the Olson database contains the correct information.*

`conda-forge`'s build of `r-base` differs from R's default, and passes `--without-internal-tzcode` on macOS ([code link](https://github.com/conda-forge/r-base-feedstock/blob/c4525e474a162db58b978f5ed83f051b8ab07c78/recipe/build-r-base.sh#L529)).

According to `git blame`, it's been like that since the commit that first introduced that recipe 7 years ago ([blame link](https://github.com/conda-forge/r-base-feedstock/blame/c4525e474a162db58b978f5ed83f051b8ab07c78/recipe/build-r-base.sh#L529)).

### References

These conclusions are supported by investigation @MichaelChirico and I recently did into a change I proposed for `{data.table}`'s feedstock:

* https://github.com/conda-forge/r-data.table-feedstock/pull/35#issuecomment-1872477543
* https://github.com/Rdatatable/data.table/pull/5867

Not sure, but this might be the cause of what @jdblischak experienced in https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/pull/5405#issuecomment-373718358.

Note that the IANA timezone database (the one R also bundles) is packaged in 2 different formats for `conda-forge`:

* https://github.com/conda-forge/tzdata-feedstock
* https://github.com/conda-forge/r-tzdb-feedstock

### Notes for Reviewers

I appreciate that any change to how `r-base` is compiled can potentially have a huge impact. I'm not as familiar with `conda-forge` as others here, but I'd be happy to help however I can with other work to make this change happen or provide more evidence that it should happen.

Thanks for your time and consideration.

running `{data.table}`'s unit tests as a part of builds here. This ~introduces 0 new build-time dependencies and~ offers significantly higher coverage of potential build problems than the current testing strategy, which is "check that `library(data.table)` runs without error". Let's see what the change in build time is when CI runs, but assuming it's on the order of 0-3 minutes, I think it's worth it in exchange for stronger guarantees about correctness of the build here. Especially for a project like `{data.table}`, which is depended on by hundreds of other R packages (see "reverse imports" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html). ## How I tested this Locally on my mac (intel chip, macOS 12.2.1, 8GB RAM), ran the following on this branch: ```shell docker run \ --rm \ -v $(pwd):/opt/data.table \ -w /opt/data.table \ --entrypoint="" \ -it condaforge/linux-anvil-cos7-x86_64 \ bash conda build . ``` Saw that build succeed, and this output confirming that the tests ran successsfully: ```text All 8997 tests (last 2163) in tests/tests.Rraw.bz2 completed ok in 25.8s elapsed (28.2s cpu) + exit 0 Resource usage statistics from testing r-data.table: Process count: 4 CPU time: Sys=0:00:00.8, User=0:00:27.6 Memory: 177.3M Disk usage: 16B Time elapsed: 0:00:28.1 ``` For details on that `test.data.table()` function, see https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/reference/test.data.table.html?q=test.data#ref-examples. Feel free to push to the bot's branch to update this PR if needed. 2. The bot will almost always only open one PR per version. 3. The bot will stop issuing PRs if more than 3 version bump PRs generated by the bot are open. If you don't want to package a particular version please close the PR. 4. If you want these PRs to be merged automatically, make an issue with @conda-forge-admin,`please add bot automerge` in the title and merge the resulting PR. This command will add our bot automerge feature to your feedstock. 5. If this PR was opened in error or needs to be updated please add the `bot-rerun` label to this PR. The bot will close this PR and schedule another one. If you do not have permissions to add this label, you can use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerun bot in a PR comment to have the `conda-forge-admin` add it for you. Dependency Analysis -------------------- Please note that this analysis is **highly experimental**. The aim here is to make maintenance easier by inspecting the package's dependencies. Importantly this analysis does not support optional dependencies, please double check those before making changes. Proposes fixing what appears to be a typo in the documentation. The README refers to paths like `pkgs32` as "now allowed", but those paths appear to actually NOT be allowed in `conda` packages:

https://github.com/conda/conda-verify/blob/23a018ba4b73b1d61a3d29098c2174fd49c4b9ed/conda_verify/checks.py#L354-L355

Thanks for your time and consideration.

### Checklist - did you ...

- [x] Add / update necessary tests?

`conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Summary This PR proposes running `{data.table}`'s unit tests as a part of builds here. This introduces 0 new build-time dependencies and offers significantly higher coverage of potential build problems than the current testing strategy, which is "check that `library(data.table)` runs without error". Let's see what the change in build time is when CI runs, but assuming it's on the order of 0-3 minutes, I think it's worth it in exchange for stronger guarantees about correctness of the build here. Especially for a project like `{data.table}`, which is depended on by hundreds of other R packages (see "reverse imports" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html). ## How I tested this Locally on my mac (intel chip, macOS 12.2.1, 8GB RAM), ran the following on this branch: ```shell docker run \ --rm \ -v $(pwd):/opt/data.table \ -w /opt/data.table \ --entrypoint="" \ -it condaforge/linux-anvil-cos7-x86_64 \ bash conda build . ``` Saw that build succeed, and this output confirming that the tests ran successsfully: ```text All 8997 tests (last 2163) in tests/tests.Rraw.bz2 completed ok in 25.8s elapsed (28.2s cpu) + exit 0 Resource usage statistics from testing r-data.table: Process count: 4 CPU time: Sys=0:00:00.8, User=0:00:27.6 Memory: 177.3M Disk usage: 16B Time elapsed: 0:00:28.1 ``` For details on that `test.data.table()` function, see https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/reference/test.data.table.html?q=test.data#ref-examples. `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Summary This PR proposes running `{data.table}`'s unit tests as a part of builds here. This introduces 0 new build-time dependencies and offers significantly higher coverage of potential build problems than the current testing strategy, which is "check that `library(data.table)` runs without error". Let's see what the change in build time is when CI runs, but assuming it's on the order of 0-3 minutes, I think it's worth it in exchange for stronger guarantees about correctness of the build here. Especially for a project like `{data.table}`, which is depended on by hundreds of other R packages (see "reverse imports" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html). ## How I tested this Locally on my mac (intel chip, macOS 12.2.1, 8GB RAM), ran the following on this branch: ```shell docker run \ --rm \ -v $(pwd):/opt/data.table \ -w /opt/data.table \ --entrypoint="" \ -it condaforge/linux-anvil-cos7-x86_64 \ bash conda build . ``` Saw that build succeed, and this output confirming that the tests ran successsfully: ```text All 8997 tests (last 2163) in tests/tests.Rraw.bz2 completed ok in 25.8s elapsed (28.2s cpu) + exit 0 Resource usage statistics from testing r-data.table: Process count: 4 CPU time: Sys=0:00:00.8, User=0:00:27.6 Memory: 177.3M Disk usage: 16B Time elapsed: 0:00:28.1 ``` For details on that `test.data.table()` function, see https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/reference/test.data.table.html?q=test.data#ref-examples. Thanks very much for your time and * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [x] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [x] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Summary This PR proposes running `{data.table}`'s unit tests as a part of builds here. This introduces 0 new build-time dependencies and offers significantly higher coverage of potential build problems than the current testing strategy, which is "check that `library(data.table)` runs without error". Let's see what the change in build time is when CI runs, but assuming it's on the order of 0-3 minutes, I think it's worth it in exchange for stronger guarantees about correctness of the build here. Especially for a project like `{data.table}`, which is depended on by hundreds of other R packages (see "reverse imports" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html). ## How I tested this Locally on my mac (intel chip, macOS 12.2.1, 8GB RAM), ran the following on this branch: ```shell docker run \ --rm \ -v $(pwd):/opt/data.table \ -w /opt/data.table \ --entrypoint="" \ -it condaforge/linux-anvil-cos7-x86_64 \ bash conda build . ``` Saw that build succeed, and this output confirming that the tests ran successsfully: ```text All 8997 tests (last 2163) in tests/tests.Rraw.bz2 completed ok in 25.8s elapsed (28.2s cpu) + exit 0 Resource usage statistics from testing r-data.table: Process count: 4 CPU time: Sys=0:00:00.8, User=0:00:27.6 Memory: 177.3M Disk usage: 16B Time elapsed: 0:00:28.1 ``` For details on that `test.data.table()` function, see https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/reference/test.data.table.html?q=test.data#ref-examples. Thanks very much for your time and * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [x] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [x] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Summary This PR proposes running `{data.table}`'s unit tests as a part of builds here. This introduces 0 new build-time dependencies and offers significantly higher coverage of potential build problems than the current testing strategy, which is "check that `library(data.table)` runs without error". Let's see what the change in build time is when CI runs, but assuming it's on the order of 0-3 minutes, I think it's worth it in exchange for stronger guarantees about correctness of the build here. Especially for a project like `{data.table}`, which is depended on by hundreds of other R packages (see "reverse imports" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html). ## How I tested this Locally on my mac (intel chip, macOS 12.2.1, 8GB RAM), ran the following on this branch: ```shell docker run \ --rm \ -v $(pwd):/opt/data.table \ -w /opt/data.table \ --entrypoint="" \ -it condaforge/linux-anvil-cos7-x86_64 \ bash conda build . ``` Saw that build succeed, and this output confirming that the tests ran successsfully: ```text All 8997 tests (last 2163) in tests/tests.Rraw.bz2 completed ok in 25.8s elapsed (28.2s cpu) + exit 0 Resource usage statistics from testing r-data.table: Process count: 4 CPU time: Sys=0:00:00.8, User=0:00:27.6 Memory: 177.3M Disk usage: 16B Time elapsed: 0:00:28.1 ``` For details on that `test.data.table()` function, see https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/reference/test.data.table.html?q=test.data#ref-examples. * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [x] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [x] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. ## Summary This PR proposes running `{data.table}`'s unit tests as a part of builds here. This introduces 0 new build-time dependencies and offers significantly higher coverage of potential build problems than the current testing strategy, which is "check that `library(data.table)` runs without error". Let's see what the change in build time is when CI runs, but assuming it's on the order of 0-3 minutes, I think it's worth it in exchange for stronger guarantees about correctness of the build here. Especially for a project like `{data.table}`, which is depended on by hundreds of other R packages (see "reverse imports" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html). ## How I tested this Locally on my mac (intel chip, macOS 12.2.1, 8GB RAM), ran the following on this branch: ```shell docker run \ --rm \ -v $(pwd):/opt/data.table \ -w /opt/data.table \ --entrypoint="" \ -it condaforge/linux-anvil-cos7-x86_64 \ bash conda build . ``` Saw that build succeed, and this output confirming that the tests ran successsfully: ```text All 8997 tests (last 2163) in tests/tests.Rraw.bz2 completed ok in 25.8s elapsed (28.2s cpu) + exit 0 Resource usage statistics from testing r-data.table: Process count: 4 CPU time: Sys=0:00:00.8, User=0:00:27.6 Memory: 177.3M Disk usage: 16B Time elapsed: 0:00:28.1 ``` For details on that `test.data.table()` function, see https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/reference/test.data.table.html?q=test.data#ref-examples. Thanks very much for your time and
